other_observers: None; First found by a newpaper photographer in Chaffee Co. on 12/28/2005; hopefully, Sherrie York's documentation mentions the man's name, as I do not recall it. Sherrie York then confirmed the sighting, took a few pictures, and emailed them to
specific_location: On farm pond on S side of CR 160, <1 mi SE of intersection with CR 161, NW of Salida
description: These were all adults and were very large, all white-plumaged birds, with black bills. They held their necks without a kink at the base. The bills were very long and met the forehead in a long, continuous slant, without the short step typical of Tundra Swan (TUSW). All lacked yellow patches at the base of the maxilla and all sported orangish-red cutting edges. The eyes were not isolated from the bill, as the head's feathering did not pinch them off as in TUSW. Their behaviors suggested that the six birds were comprised of three mated pairs.Behaviors: Sleeping, loafing, preening, and foragingCall: None heardPlumage: Definitive
